reviewBasis: Newest match first; recordings inside a match are sorted by length.

mainFeedback.title: Samira: stop giving wins back
mainFeedback.focus: Honest read: your biggest leak is not damage. It is refusing to cash out: staying at lethal HP, re-fighting after winning, and chasing sideways when structure is already open.
mainFeedback.rule: If the next action does not create HP safety, wave crash, turret, or nexus pressure, do not take it.
mainFeedback.nextRep: Next game: cash out before the second fight.
mainFeedback.whyTrust: The clips show the same leak in three forms: lethal-HP lane stay, low-health re-fight, and open-base drift. That is specific enough to practice immediately.
mainFeedback.pattern: You already find fights. The coachable problem is making the second decision boring enough to actually win: reset, crash, hit tower, hit nexus, then fight again only if it protects that payout.
checklist: First win becomes wave or structure.
Open base means no sideways chase.
mainFeedback.reviewLimit: Replay review is based on sampled frames and visible state, not raw inputs or full cooldown telemetry.

detectedChampions.name: Samira
detectedChampions.evidence: Manual storyboard review of the May 18 full recording: early lane death at lethal HP, later kills and turret/base conversion after grouping.
detectedChampions.improvement: Before committing, know what the win buys: crash, plate, tower, dragon move, recall, or end.

recordings.title: full review 05
recordings.feedbackTitle: Stop Greedy Lane Stays on Low HP After Winning
recordings.feedback: Mistake: Staying in lane after early fights at lethal HP instead of resetting. Fix: Immediately recall and reset after pushing the wave when you’re low after a fight, instead of staying for more minions or plates.
recordings.gameDetail: Alan’s Samira takes multiple risky low-health post-fight lane stays after early skirmishes (ex: 1:35, 3:51, 4:11) and repeatedly sticks around with under 20% HP to farm or apply pressure instead of recalling after pushing. This led to preventable deaths (1:56) and several situations where getting greedy risked a shutdown, especially with enemy support and jungler shown or missing. Even after winning an all-in or pushing out the wave, the pattern is to farm through lethal territory rather than prioritizing a safe reset and spending gold when ahead. In harder games, always prioritize a solid recall to lock in your lead and deny free returns.
recordings.eventEvidence: At 1:35 and 3:51 Samira is low HP but stays to farm or trade; at 1:56 Samira is killed after such a stay, and at 4:11 seen below 20% HP still playing up with enemies visible or possibly approaching.
recordings.goodThing: Aggressive all-ins were timed well with minion waves pushing under tower, leading to early kill pressure.
recordings.whyTrust: If you reset after fights, you’ll avoid throwing shutdown gold or tempo, making Samira’s snowball consistent and making sure you show up to future skirmishes with items and full health.
recordings.evidence: Multiple frames (1:35, 3:51, 4:11) show Samira farming with low HP and not backing post-fight.
recordings.pattern: Early kill involvement is immediately followed by greedy wave clear or tower pressure at killable HP, instead of resetting. This habit wastes your early pressure and puts easy gold on the map for the enemy.
recordings.diamondRule: Never expose a shutdown by staying on the map under 30% HP after an all-in or tower dive, especially as a snowball ADC.
recordings.drill: Next game, after every fight or chunk that leaves you below 30% HP, push the wave and immediately recall—no exceptions for farm or plates.
timeline: 01:35 - Samira low HP post-fight still farming wave
04:11 - Lane posture with minimal HP and gold unspent
nuance: Samira is full committing to early fights, getting kills but not recalling after.
Frames show Samira repeatedly contesting farm and lane space at under 200 HP.
Gold count is climbing without a reset, risking value if killed.
Enemy support/jungler presence not accounted for when sticking around.
After winning trades, tempo is lost to respawns due to greedy lane stays.
recordings.reviewLimit: Frames are missing some transitions and vision checks, so the exact enemy approach awareness can’t be fully confirmed, but the low-HP lane stays are clear and repeatedly visible.

recordings.title: highlight 13
recordings.feedback: Mistake: over-learning this clip would make you throw; the same dash is bad if it starts without wave, teammate body, or nexus pressure. Fix: full send only when every reset moves toward the payout.
recordings.gameDetail: At 14:21 the fight is already in their base, not in river or a side lane: allies and minions are in front, enemy defenders are packed near nexus, and Samira is close enough to chain resets without walking into fog. At 14:27 the first two kills land and the play becomes a real cleanup instead of a random chase. At 14:33 the chain reaches triple, at 14:38 the quadra happens beside the nexus area, and at 14:41 the penta lands because the fight never leaves the ending window. Learn this exactly: the all-in is good when the map is already ending; outside that condition, cash out first.
recordings.eventEvidence: 14:21 fight starts inside the enemy base with multiple enemy defenders still alive; 14:27 double kill; 14:33 triple; 14:38 quadra near nexus; 14:41 penta as the base remains open.
recordings.goodThing: The strong part is the finish discipline: once the enemy line collapses, Samira stays with the ally/minion push and cleans through the defenders instead of drifting sideways.
recordings.whyTrust: The penta is not random: the clip shows the conditions that made it valid, with allies and minions already in base and every reset moving toward nexus.
recordings.evidence: Manual storyboard review of the May 20 highlight: visible base fight from 14:21 to 14:41, double/triple/quadra/penta sequence, and enemy base already open.
recordings.pattern: Samira's best-looking fight here works because the map already gives the exit and the payout. The dangerous habit would be copying the same depth when the fight is not already a base finish.
recordings.diamondRule: All-in only when the reset path and the payout are the same direction.
recordings.drill: Before E/R, ask: does every reset move me toward nexus, tower, dragon, Baron, or safety?
timeline: 14:21 - Samira is already inside the enemy base with several defenders alive and allied pressure in front.
14:27 - The first two kills land, turning the base fight into a cleanup instead of a loose chase.
14:33 - The reset chain reaches triple while the wave and allies still hold the base area.
14:38 - The quadra happens near nexus instead of in a side lane or fog pocket.
14:41 - The penta lands with the enemy base open, which is the correct payoff condition.
nuance: The clip starts after the decision to enter, so it cannot prove whether the first click was disciplined.
The visible reason the play works is base geometry plus allies and minions, not just raw damage.
The penta is good evidence that the cleanup mechanics are there.
The next improvement is knowing when not to copy this same depth outside a finish window.
recordings.reviewLimit: The Riot highlight is only about 22 seconds, so the review claims the visible base fight and does not invent the earlier setup.

recordings.title: full review 04
recordings.feedback: Mistake: you chase sideways after the base is already open. Fix: stand behind the first body in and hit the structure as soon as space is made.
recordings.gameDetail: Honest read: the useful footage starts after a desktop-capture section, and the lesson is still clear. Around 12:10 Samira is low below the enemy base entrance while allies are already fighting inside the cracked base; the next valuable movement is backward-to-safe or forward-to-structure with the team, not a solo side angle. Around 13:05 the map slows down: Mordekaiser walks away mid, an ally is channeling recall, and Samira is full HP with the lane already open, which is exactly the moment to choose wave/base route before another target appears. Around 13:15 the camera follows Mordekaiser again and Samira casts Q/Flair into him from close range; the spell connects, but the important part is that the fight is sideways while the open base is still the real payout. Around 14:19 the clean version finally shows up: multiple enemies are dead, minions are in the base, Samira is behind the front body, and the team is hitting the ending area instead of wandering after another duel.
recordings.eventEvidence: 12:10 low Samira below the open enemy base entrance; 13:15 Q/Flair into Mordekaiser during a sideways mid fight; 14:19 team and minions are back inside the base to end.
recordings.goodThing: You did group back toward the base and finish the game instead of letting the push fully dissolve.
recordings.whyTrust: The win came from returning to the open base, not from a cleaner combo. Your damage was not the limiting factor; target discipline was.
recordings.evidence: Review clip frames with visible game clock: base pressure near 12:10, Mordekaiser Q/Flair around 13:15, final base push around 14:19.
recordings.pattern: The winning pressure was already available. The mistake is treating a nearby champion like the win condition after the actual win condition is already open.
recordings.diamondRule: If a base structure is available, a chase is wrong unless it directly removes the body blocking the structure.
recordings.drill: Next game: when inhibitor or nexus is open, say front line, structure, no chase.
timeline: 12:10 - Samira is low below the enemy base entrance while allies are already inside the cracked base.
13:15 - Samira Q/Flair follows Mordekaiser mid instead of immediately routing the open lane back to base.
14:19 - The team has minions inside the base and Samira is behind the first body, which is the clean ending shape.
nuance: The most useful footage is the late-game base decision, not the scoreboard line.
The mistake is drifting after the base route is already available.
Samira's cleanest role here is finisher behind the first body, not fight starter.
The recording still contains desktop-capture segments from before the window-only recorder fix.
recordings.reviewLimit: The clip has desktop-capture segments mixed with game footage, so the narrative only claims the visible game moments.

recordings.title: full review 03
recordings.feedback: Mistake: you keep playing for another fight after the first win. Fix: cash out wave, tower, reset, or end before touching another champion.
recordings.gameDetail: Honest read: this is improved, but the same leak keeps coming back after the good moment. Around 1:24 Samira is fighting under the friendly bot tower with Jinx and Braum still on screen; the movement is forward into a contested lane pocket while the safer value is to thin the wave, hold the tower line, and make them walk into you. Around 7:58 the good version happens: the bot push is already under enemy tower, the wave is with you, and the won pressure turns into turret damage instead of another random chase. Around 10:32 Samira is low in bot lane while Braum and Jinx can still answer, so every extra step forward is a shutdown invitation unless the next click is reset or structure. Around 12:44 the punishment is visible: the fight is still extended near the enemy side, crowd control lands, and the screen shows a shutdown instead of a clean cashout. The lesson is not to fight less; it is to make the first won fight buy the wave, turret, reset, or end before accepting the second one.
recordings.eventEvidence: 1:24 contested fight under friendly bot tower; 7:58 won bot push becomes turret damage; 10:32 low-health lane stay; 12:44 crowd control and shutdown punish the extended fight.
recordings.goodThing: The good part was real: you turned one bot-side win into turret pressure instead of only chasing kills.
recordings.whyTrust: The same game shows both sides: turret conversion is the right version, and the later low-health re-fight is how stronger opponents get shutdown gold back.
recordings.evidence: Manual storyboard review of the May 18 8:10 PM game: early bot pressure, double-kill conversion, turret take, inhibitor take, and nexus pressure.
recordings.pattern: The damage is already there. The leak is the second decision: after the first win, you often accept another fight before the map payout is locked.
recordings.diamondRule: First win buys wave, tower, reset, or end before the next fight is allowed.
recordings.drill: After the first kill or forced recall, say cash out before moving forward.
timeline: 1:24 - Early bot fight is still near friendly tower with both lane enemies able to answer.
7:58 - Bot pressure becomes turret, which is the good conversion.
10:32-12:44 - Low-health extended fighting replaces the clean cashout/reset.
nuance: At 3:40 the bot fight becomes a double kill because Samira stays near the wave and ally pressure.
At 7:58 the won lane becomes turret gold instead of more random fighting.
At 10:32 Samira is low while Braum is still present; that is a cashout moment, not a retest moment.
At 12:44 Samira is still in the extended lane fight with enemy tools active; that habit gets punished fast.
Beginner bot games reward extra fighting, so the transferable skill is conversion first, second fight only if it protects the payout.
recordings.reviewLimit: Manual review used sampled replay frames and log timing, not raw inputs or full cooldown telemetry.

recordings.title: full review 02
recordings.feedbackTitle: You stay when one hit kills you
recordings.feedback: Mistake: you keep playing the wave when one auto or spell kills you. Fix: give the wave and recall before the lane turns into a death timer.
recordings.gameDetail: Honest read: this is not a mechanics issue; it is a health-gate issue. At 2:43 Samira is under bot tower with the enemy wave arriving, Ashe still visible to the right, and enough missing HP that the lane should already be treated as dangerous instead of playable. At 3:27 the real mistake is clearer: Samira is still beside the bot wave at lethal health, the camera and movement are staying with minions, and the recall path has not been taken even though one clean enemy touch can turn the wave into a death timer. At 4:57 the good version is visible after the reset: Samira is back on the map with enough HP to walk forward, Q/auto from range, and choose the next trade instead of begging not to be hit. By 14:28 the team is ending with Samira alive around the base fight, which proves the champion damage is not the blocker. The blocker is staying in lane after the health bar says the next decision should be recall.
recordings.eventEvidence: 2:43 Samira is under bot tower with Ashe still visible and the wave arriving; 3:27 Samira is still in lane at lethal health; 4:57 reset gives enough HP to re-enter; 14:28 the later team ending works.
recordings.goodThing: After the bad early health decision, you still got back into the game and converted later pressure into the ending push.
recordings.whyTrust: This is not vague advice: the reviewed frames show the exact one-hit-health lane stay, and removing that habit protects the aggression that already works later.
recordings.evidence: Manual storyboard review of the May 18 full recording: early lane death at lethal HP, later kills and turret/base conversion after grouping.
recordings.pattern: The new game is better at converting once Samira has items, but the early lane still has a lethal-HP greed point: staying for the wave while one hit from death turns a manageable recall into a death timer.
recordings.diamondRule: Below one enemy auto or spell, the wave is no longer the objective; reset first, then play the next item spike.
recordings.drill: At low HP, say one hit kills me and recall unless the enemy bot lane is dead or fully gone.
timeline: 2:43 - Samira is under bot tower with Ashe still visible and the wave arriving.
3:27 - Samira is still in lane at lethal health instead of already reset.
4:57 - Samira is back with enough HP to re-enter and choose the next trade.
14:28 - The later ending works, which makes the early health-gate mistake the cleaner thing to remove.
nuance: At 2:43 the wave is not worth more than the death timer.
At 3:27 the lane is still being played from one-hit health, which better enemies punish immediately.
Later frames show better conversion: kills become turret pressure, inhibitor pressure, and nexus pressure.
The improvement is to keep the later aggression while removing the early death that delays the first real item window.
recordings.reviewLimit: Manual review used sampled replay frames and close-up storyboard sheets, not raw inputs or complete cooldown telemetry.

recordings.title: full review 01
recordings.feedback: The full-game read says damage is enough; the next useful rep is ending won fights through wave, tower, dragon, Baron, nexus, or recall.
recordings.gameDetail: This full review shows Samira can already create kills, so the climb gap is what happens right after the first win. The next useful rep is converting the won fight into wave, tower, objective, nexus, or recall before the next fight gives shutdown gold back.
recordings.eventEvidence: The full-game result shows high kill creation with too many deaths, which points to conversion and shutdown protection rather than raw damage.
recordings.goodThing: You are finding fights and creating damage pressure; the fix is cashing those wins out cleaner.
recordings.whyTrust: A 16/10 Samira can already create leads; reducing deaths after wins keeps shutdown gold and turns mechanics into rank pressure.
recordings.pattern: The carry score says damage is available, so the rank leak is likely conversion after the first winning moment.
recordings.diamondRule: After a won fight, take the guaranteed payout before looking for the next fight.
recordings.drill: Say the payout out loud after every kill: wave, tower, dragon, Baron, nexus, or recall.
nuance: High kills only matter when the map state changes.
Shutdown deaths after a win erase the lead Samira already created.
recordings.reviewLimit: The review is based on sampled frames, not full input/cooldown telemetry.

recordings.title: highlight 12
recordings.feedback: At inhib or nexus, the next useful rep is ending as soon as the structure is available.
recordings.gameDetail: Once the base structure is available, kills are only a way to clear space for hitting it. The next useful rep is moving the cursor to the structure first, because structure damage is the lowest-variance conversion of the won fight.
recordings.goodThing: The useful positive sign is that Samira is creating pressure; the next step is making that pressure safer and more repeatable.
recordings.whyTrust: This is reliable because structures are guaranteed progress; extra fighting after the base is open adds variance without adding win condition.
recordings.pattern: Base fights become lower value once the structure is open.
recordings.diamondRule: Hit the structure as soon as the fight is won.
recordings.drill: After a base kill, move the cursor to the structure first.
nuance: The structure is the lowest-variance way to convert the lead.
recordings.reviewLimit: The review is based on sampled frames, not full input/cooldown telemetry.

recordings.title: highlight 05
recordings.feedback: Let Q, autos, and W collect cooldowns before entering; Samira should clean the fight, not start it blind.
recordings.gameDetail: This clip points to Samira's best entry shape: use the edge of the fight to collect cooldown information, then dash when the reset path is real. The next useful rep is staying aggressive while delaying the full commit by one beat.
recordings.goodThing: The useful positive sign is that Samira is creating pressure; the next step is making that pressure safer and more repeatable.
recordings.whyTrust: This lowers anxiety without lowering aggression because edge play gathers real cooldown evidence before the all-in.
recordings.pattern: The edge of the fight gives Samira information without spending the dash.
recordings.diamondRule: Fight the first second from range, then enter after the enemy spends tools.
recordings.drill: Q-auto once before committing unless the target is already isolated and lethal.
nuance: This is still aggressive because it prepares the reset instead of canceling the fight.
recordings.reviewLimit: The review is based on sampled frames, not full input/cooldown telemetry.

recordings.title: highlight 09
recordings.feedback: Catching side farm is fine; leave toward teammates unless mid has priority or three enemies are visible.
recordings.gameDetail: Side farm is useful income until it disconnects the fed carry from the next map play. The next useful rep is taking the wave, then immediately pathing back through safe vision unless enemy positions are visible.
recordings.goodThing: The useful positive sign is that Samira is creating pressure; the next step is making that pressure safer and more repeatable.
recordings.whyTrust: This turns side farm into safe income instead of isolation, which is the difference between carrying with gold and dying with gold.
recordings.pattern: Side farm is useful until it isolates the fed carry from the next map play.
recordings.diamondRule: Catch the wave, then leave unless the enemy positions are visible.
recordings.drill: Take the wave and immediately path back through safe vision.
nuance: Gold only helps if the next movement keeps the map connected.
recordings.reviewLimit: The review is based on sampled frames, not full input/cooldown telemetry.

recordings.title: highlight 02
recordings.feedbackTitle: Name the CC before going in
recordings.feedback: Before E/R, identify the one spell that cancels the play; enter only after it is spent, blocked by W, or aimed elsewhere.
recordings.gameDetail: This clip is about the spell that can stop Samira before the reset starts. The next useful rep is entering only after that crowd-control spell is spent, blocked, or aimed elsewhere, which keeps aggression while removing the easiest shutdown path.
recordings.goodThing: The useful positive sign is that Samira is creating pressure; the next step is making that pressure safer and more repeatable.
recordings.whyTrust: This is the highest-value check because one untracked stun, root, knock-up, or hook turns Samira's whole reset plan off.
recordings.pattern: The dangerous moment is entering before the enemy's fight-stopping spell is accounted for.
recordings.diamondRule: No full commit until the one spell that stops Samira is gone or aimed elsewhere.
recordings.drill: Name one enemy CC spell before every fight.
nuance: The check is not passive; it preserves aggression by waiting for the real opening.
recordings.reviewLimit: The review is based on sampled frames, not full input/cooldown telemetry.

recordings.title: highlight 08
recordings.feedback: If a teammate dies nearby, count visible enemies before spending E to rescue a fight.
recordings.gameDetail: This moment is about the rescue instinct that can pull Samira into a fight already lost on numbers. The next useful rep is counting visible enemies before using E toward trouble, because skipping a bad rescue protects carry gold.
recordings.goodThing: The useful positive sign is that Samira is creating pressure; the next step is making that pressure safer and more repeatable.
recordings.whyTrust: Counting visible enemies gives anxiety a concrete check; if the numbers are bad, skipping the rescue is discipline, not fear.
recordings.pattern: Rescue instincts can drag Samira into a fight that is already numerically lost.
recordings.diamondRule: Count visible enemies before answering a teammate death.
recordings.drill: Say the number count before using E toward a fight.
nuance: Skipping the rescue can be the correct carry decision when the fight is already gone.
recordings.reviewLimit: The review is based on sampled frames, not full input/cooldown telemetry.

recordings.title: highlight 04
recordings.feedback: After the first kill or forced recall, crash wave first, then choose plate, dragon move, reset, or support roam.
recordings.gameDetail: The lane win matters only if the enemy loses minions, plates, tempo, or map space after it. The next useful rep is touching the wave before looking for more fighting, because the crash makes every next option safer.
recordings.goodThing: The useful positive sign is that Samira is creating pressure; the next step is making that pressure safer and more repeatable.
recordings.whyTrust: This is how a won lane becomes rank progress: the enemy loses minions and map space even if no second kill happens.
recordings.pattern: Lane wins need to become minion loss, plate pressure, or objective tempo.
recordings.diamondRule: Crash before chasing the second reward.
recordings.drill: After a bot kill, touch the wave before looking for another fight.
nuance: The crash makes the next play safer because the enemy loses time answering the wave.
recordings.reviewLimit: The review is based on sampled frames, not full input/cooldown telemetry.

recordings.title: highlight 07
recordings.feedback: After a tower or multi-kill, leave with the shutdown value instead of retesting the fight.
recordings.gameDetail: Once Samira has the payout, the risk changes: another fight is no longer worth the same as the gold already held. The next useful rep is leaving with shutdown value unless the next objective is already free.
recordings.goodThing: The useful positive sign is that Samira is creating pressure; the next step is making that pressure safer and more repeatable.
recordings.whyTrust: This is worth practicing because it does not ask for less carry pressure; it keeps the carry gold from being handed back.
recordings.pattern: The risk shifts after Samira has gold; the next death is more expensive than the next kill is valuable.
recordings.diamondRule: Protect shutdown gold after the first payout.
recordings.drill: Recall after the payout unless an objective is already free.
nuance: This preserves carry pressure by making the next item arrive sooner.
recordings.reviewLimit: The review is based on sampled frames, not full input/cooldown telemetry.

recordings.title: highlight 03
recordings.feedback: A low target past vision is not free; shove the wave or take plate unless the next enemy position is known.
recordings.gameDetail: The low target is tempting, but fog removes the information needed to know whether the next dash is a reset or a bait. The next useful rep is stopping at vision loss and taking wave or plate unless enemy positions are known.
recordings.goodThing: The useful positive sign is that Samira is creating pressure; the next step is making that pressure safer and more repeatable.
recordings.whyTrust: The rule is trustworthy because fog removes the information Samira needs to decide whether the next E is a reset or a bait.
recordings.pattern: Chasing past fog turns a won trade into an information gamble.
recordings.diamondRule: Stop at fog unless the next enemy positions are known.
recordings.drill: When the target leaves vision, look at wave and objective before moving forward.
nuance: Fog is not just darkness; it removes reset and exit information.
recordings.reviewLimit: The review is based on sampled frames, not full input/cooldown telemetry.

recordings.title: highlight 11
recordings.feedback: Hold the edge until enemy CC is used so Samira stays the finisher instead of the target.
recordings.gameDetail: Chokes punish the first champion in, especially when the enemy still has the spell that stops Samira. The next useful rep is being second in at jungle walls so the dash starts after the first cooldown exchange.
recordings.goodThing: The useful positive sign is that Samira is creating pressure; the next step is making that pressure safer and more repeatable.
recordings.whyTrust: This preserves the aggression while removing the single easiest way enemies stop Samira.
recordings.pattern: Chokes punish the first champion in; Samira gets more value as the second entry.
recordings.diamondRule: Second in at jungle walls unless the enemy CC is already gone.
recordings.drill: Pause at the choke edge and enter after the first cooldown exchange.
nuance: The pause is a setup for resets, not hesitation.
recordings.reviewLimit: The review is based on sampled frames, not full input/cooldown telemetry.

recordings.title: highlight 10
recordings.feedback: When an enemy catches a wave, pressure the objective first; take the duel only with ult, summoner info, and a walk-out.
recordings.gameDetail: The side duel can look like the game, but objective pressure forces the enemy to answer on worse terms. The next useful rep is making the map ask the question first, then dueling only when ult, summoners, and exit are known.
recordings.goodThing: The useful positive sign is that Samira is creating pressure; the next step is making that pressure safer and more repeatable.
recordings.whyTrust: Objectives force the enemy to answer on your terms; random duels make the game hinge on mechanics under uncertainty.
recordings.pattern: A side duel can distract from the objective that would make the enemy respond.
recordings.drill: Ping or path to the objective before matching the side target.
nuance: Making the enemy answer the map lowers the need for perfect mechanics.

recordings.title: highlight 01
recordings.feedback: Before committing, know what the win buys: crash, plate, tower, dragon move, recall, or end.
recordings.gameDetail: The useful question in this clip is not whether Samira can win the fight; it is what the fight buys immediately after. The next useful rep is naming the payout before the commit so pressure becomes a wave crash, plate, tower, objective move, recall, or end.
recordings.goodThing: The useful positive sign is that Samira is creating pressure; the next step is making that pressure safer and more repeatable.
recordings.whyTrust: Strong ADC games climb by turning pressure into tempo; a kill with no payout is just a higher-risk fight.
recordings.pattern: The fight needs a planned map payout before the commit.
recordings.diamondRule: Commit only when the win has an immediate conversion path.
recordings.drill: Name the payout before pressing the engage button.
